title: Lynnewood Hall

infobox.value: 920 Spring Ave., [Elkins Park, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania "Elkins Park, Pennsylvania"), United States
Lynnewood Hall Preservation Foundation (purchased from First Korean Church of New York in 2023)

is a 110-room [Neoclassical Revival](/wiki/Neoclassical_Revival "Neoclassical Revival") mansion in [Elkins Park, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania "Elkins Park, Pennsylvania"). It was designed by the architect [Horace Trumbauer](/wiki/Horace_Trumbauer "Horace Trumbauer") for the industrialist [Peter A. B. Widener](/wiki/Peter_Arrell_Brown_Widener "Peter Arrell Brown Widener") and built between 1897 and 1899. Lynnewood Hall is the second largest surviving [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") mansion in the United States and once housed one of the most significant art collections in American history, amassed by Peter and his son [Joseph E. Widener](/wiki/Joseph_E._Widener "Joseph E. Widener") and donated to the [National Gallery of Art](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Art "National Gallery of Art") in 1942.

**Lynnewood Hall** is a 110-room [Neoclassical Revival](/wiki/Neoclassical_Revival "Neoclassical Revival") mansion in [Elkins Park, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania "Elkins Park, Pennsylvania"). It was designed by the architect [Horace Trumbauer](/wiki/Horace_Trumbauer "Horace Trumbauer") for the industrialist [Peter A. B. Widener](/wiki/Peter_Arrell_Brown_Widener "Peter Arrell Brown Widener") and built between 1897 and 1899. Lynnewood Hall is the second largest surviving [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") mansion in the United States and once housed one of the most significant art collections in American history, amassed by Peter and his son [Joseph E. Widener](/wiki/Joseph_E._Widener "Joseph E. Widener") and donated to the [National Gallery of Art](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Art "National Gallery of Art") in 1942.

Peter Widener died at Lynnewood Hall at the age of 80 on November 6, 1915, after prolonged poor health.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He was predeceased by his elder son [George Dunton Widener](/wiki/George_Dunton_Widener "George Dunton Widener") and grandson [Harry Elkins Widener](/wiki/Harry_Elkins_Widener "Harry Elkins Widener"), both of whom died when [RMS *Titanic*](/wiki/RMS_Titanic "RMS Titanic") sank in 1912. The building changed hands a few times over the subsequent decades, with large portions of the estate grounds sold off in the 1940s. It hosted a [Bible Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Bible_Presbyterian_Church "Bible Presbyterian Church") seminary from 1952 until the late 1990s, when the property was abandoned.

In 2023, Lynnewood Hall was purchased by the Lynnewood Hall Preservation Foundation, which has announced plans to restore the house and grounds.[[2]](#cite_note-chlocal-2) The property was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

Built from [Indiana limestone](/wiki/Indiana_limestone "Indiana limestone"), the T-shaped Lynnewood Hall (dubbed "The last of the American [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles")" by Widener's grandson) measures 268 feet (82 m) long by 215 feet (66 m) deep.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In addition to 55 bedrooms, the 110-room mansion had a large art gallery, a ballroom large enough for 1,000 guests, swimming pool, wine cellars, a farm, carpentry and upholstery studios, and an electrical power plant. The estate originally encompassed 300 acres, upon 33 of which a fenced ornamental garden was constructed. The fenced area includes a gatehouse and smaller Lynnewood Lodge (also known as Conklin Hall), built in the same style as the mansion. The mansion's grounds have remained contiguous since the 1952 sale.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

A 2014 article in *[The Philadelphia Inquirer](/wiki/The_Philadelphia_Inquirer "The Philadelphia Inquirer")* described the mansion as "dripping with silk, velvet, and gilded moldings, the rooms furnished with chairs from [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV")'s palace, Persian rugs, and Chinese pottery, the halls crammed with art by [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael"), [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), [El Greco](/wiki/El_Greco "El Greco"), [van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck"), [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello")."[[6]](#cite_note-Parks-6) *[TIME](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine published an account of a lavish party held at Lynnewood Hall in 1932.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Art collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Art collection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/The_Raphael_Room_at_Lynnewood_Hall.jpg/250px-The_Raphael_Room_at_Lynnewood_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Raphael_Room_at_Lynnewood_Hall.jpg)

A 1917 portrait of the Raphael Room at Lynnewood Hall by [William Bruce Ellis Ranken](/wiki/William_Bruce_Ellis_Ranken "William Bruce Ellis Ranken")

From 1915 to 1940, the spectacular art collection at Lynnewood Hall was open to the public by appointment between June and October.

In 1942, Joseph E. Widener donated more than 2,000 sculptures, paintings, decorative art works, and porcelains to the [National Gallery of Art](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Art "National Gallery of Art"). P.A.B. Widener had originally planned for the collection to go to the Philadelphia Museum of Art. The paintings included Raphael's *[Small Cowper Madonna](/wiki/Small_Cowper_Madonna "Small Cowper Madonna")*, [Bellini](/wiki/Giovanni_Bellini "Giovanni Bellini")'s *[The Feast of the Gods](/wiki/The_Feast_of_the_Gods "The Feast of the Gods")*, eight van Dycks, two Vermeers, fourteen Rembrandts, and a series of portraits by [Gainsborough](/wiki/Thomas_Gainsborough "Thomas Gainsborough") and [Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds").[[8]](#cite_note-Quodbach-8) The sculptures included Donatello's *[David](/wiki/David_(Donatello,_bronze) "David (Donatello, bronze)")* and [Desiderio da Settignano](/wiki/Desiderio_da_Settignano "Desiderio da Settignano")'s *St John the Baptist*.[[8]](#cite_note-Quodbach-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Construction and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Construction and early years")]

By the time Horace Trumbauer was commissioned by Peter A.B. Widener to build Lynnewood Hall, Trumbauer had already designed the Widener family's Philadelphia townhome and house in New York City. Trumbauer collaborated with the French firm Carlhian et Fils to design the mansion's interiors, utilizing large amounts of salvaged European furniture, tapestries, and rooms. The mansion's 18th-century ballroom, one of the largest on the eastern seaboard, had furniture imported from Italy. Construction began in 1897, and the mansion was opened after only two years of construction with a gala held on December 19, 1899. 400 guests, mostly members of [Philadelphia high society](/wiki/Old_Philadelphians "Old Philadelphians"), attended, and the [New York Symphony](/wiki/New_York_Symphony_Orchestra "New York Symphony Orchestra") and [Johanna Gadski](/wiki/Johanna_Gadski "Johanna Gadski") provided musical accompaniment to the celebration.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The mansion was built on a property of 300 acres, and its Italian-style ornamental gardens, covering 33 acres, were originally fashioned by head gardener William Kleinheinz. The gardens included a large fountain by [Henri-Léon Gréber](/wiki/Henri-L%C3%A9on_Gr%C3%A9ber "Henri-Léon Gréber"), one of only two major surviving Gréber commissions in America.

### After Peter A.B. Widener's death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: After Peter A.B. Widener's death")]

Upon Peter A.B. Widener's death in 1915, the property passed to his son, [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_E._Widener "Joseph E. Widener"). In 1916, the gardens were redesigned in the French style by [Jacques Gréber](/wiki/Jacques_Gr%C3%A9ber "Jacques Gréber") (son of Henri-Léon), also master designer of the [Benjamin Franklin Parkway](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Parkway "Benjamin Franklin Parkway") and, later, the [1937 Paris Exhibition](/wiki/Exposition_Internationale_des_Arts_et_Techniques_dans_la_Vie_Moderne "Exposition Internationale des Arts et Techniques dans la Vie Moderne"). Boasting stables, greenhouses, a polo field, and a reservoir, the estate employed a staff of 100 at its peak.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Joseph's son, [Peter A.B. Widener II](/wiki/Peter_Arrell_Browne_Widener_II "Peter Arrell Browne Widener II"), was a passionate dog breeder, constructing extensive kennels on the property starting in 1920. As a result, the grounds were used for training [military dogs](/wiki/Dogs_in_warfare "Dogs in warfare") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Joseph Widener donated the estate's massive art collection to the National Gallery of Art in 1942, and upon his death one year later, the southern part of the estate was sold for development in 1943.[[13]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-13) The house, its gardens, and its outlying buildings were sold shortly afterwards to a private buyer who hoped to turn the property into a Protestant seminary; when this buyer defaulted on his $99,000 mortgage, the property was temporarily repossessed by the Wideners.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Sale and seminary use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sale and seminary use")]

Lynnewood Hall was purchased in 1952 by [Faith Theological Seminary](/wiki/Faith_Theological_Seminary "Faith Theological Seminary"), a Christian school of higher education headed by [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire"). The purchase price was US$192,000 (equivalent to $2,330,000 in 2025).[[15]](#cite_note-PennLive-15) The Seminary trained hundreds of ministers and Christian leaders at Lynnewood Hall for over 40 years. As the Seminary began to experience financial difficulties, it dismantled large parts of the mansion's interior, selling off what was severable.[[15]](#cite_note-PennLive-15) In 1996, the property was sold to the First Korean Church of New York, a [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)")-affiliated church with ties to Faith Theological Seminary, in a [Sheriff's Sale](/wiki/Government_auction "Government auction"). By the time of the property's purchase, all three of the remaining buildings on the property, Lynnewood Hall, Lynnewood Lodge, and the gatehouse, were in various states of dilapidation, with the gatehouse being fully abandoned. First Korean Church sought numerous times to turn the property back into a seminary and church, beginning in 1998, but failed to in a string of applications and lawsuits.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Lynnewood_Hall_-_Ballroom_looking_East%2C_LHPF_2023.jpg/250px-Lynnewood_Hall_-_Ballroom_looking_East%2C_LHPF_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lynnewood_Hall_-_Ballroom_looking_East,_LHPF_2023.jpg)

The mansion's ballroom in 2023

In 2006, in *First Korean Church of N.Y., Inc. v. Montgomery Cnty. Bd. of Assessment Appeals,* the court found that the property had not demonstrably been used for religious or educational purposes since 1998. The church continued to sell off remaining parts of the property. In 2006, Lynnewood Hall's Gréber fountain was sold at auction.[[16]](#cite_note-16) After a final February 2012 ruling that First Korean Church did not qualify for a tax exemption, Dr. Richard S. Yoon, the church's president and pastor, gave an interview to the *Philadelphia Inquirer* where he stated his intent to finally relocate the church and end the legal battle.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Further sale and restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Further sale and restoration")]

This property was on the market for $11,000,000[[18]](#cite_note-18) in May 2019. Despite multiple offers above the asking price, the home remained unsold.[[19]](#cite_note-19) A historical restoration architect estimated in 2014 that it would take about $50 million to restore the mansion to its former glory.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/LynnewoodHall_WestHall_2025.jpg/250px-LynnewoodHall_WestHall_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:LynnewoodHall_WestHall_2025.jpg)

West Hall at Lynnewood Hall, September 2025

On July 5, 2022, it was announced that Lynnewood Hall Preservation Foundation was established with the goal of acquiring "the Trumbauer-designed Widener Family Estate, a true architectural masterpiece, and see it restored to its former breathtaking glory."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On February 8, 2023, a purchase agreement was announced for the property by the foundation, with plans for the restored gardens to be open to the public as a park, and to fully restore the hall.[[2]](#cite_note-chlocal-2) On June 27, 2023, the mansion's sale was finalized for $9 million, and ownership passed to the nonprofit Lynnewood Hall Preservation Foundation.[[21]](#cite_note-PI_2023-6-30-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The purchase was primarily funded by a $9.5 million donation from board chairman Scott Bentley (a founding partner of [Bentley Systems](/wiki/Bentley_Systems "Bentley Systems")) and his wife Susan.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

A music video for the song "Come on Heartache" by the band [The Menzingers](/wiki/The_Menzingers "The Menzingers") was filmed onsite at Lynnewood Hall in September 2023.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lynnewood_Hall&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

* [National Register of Historic Places in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_in_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "National Register of Historic Places in Montgomery County, Pennsylvania")
